consensus: Components, props, and state form the foundational 'core React' that must be mastered before advanced topics
Hooks-first approach: useState, useEffect, custom hooks should precede state management libraries like Redux/Zustand
Hands-on project building (not passive tutorial consumption) is essential at every phase
Premature adoption of Redux, Next.js, or complex state libraries before core concepts is a high-risk anti-pattern
TypeScript should be introduced at an intermediate stage (not day one, not never) — typically after core React is comfortable
Next.js App Router and Server Components represent the modern production target but require understanding of client-first React first
Final deliverable should be a deployed, production-quality app (likely Next.js-based)
Create React App (CRA) is deprecated and should be explicitly avoided; Vite is the recommended replacement
Stale closures in useEffect and hook rules violations are the most common learner pitfalls
Testing should be introduced but not at the expense of core concept mastery

contradictions.details: Kimi-K2.7 recommends TypeScript after milestone M4 (mid-path). Qwen3.6-27B recommends Phase 7 (late, week ~8-10). Qwen3.6-35B-A3B recommends 'after phase 3' (early-mid, ~week 5-6). MiniMax-M3 treats TypeScript comfort as a prerequisite gate question rather than a fixed insertion point. The spread ranges from 'early prerequisite' to 'almost at the end.'
contradictions.recommended_resolution: Offer a conditional path: learners with prior TypeScript experience should use TS from day one; learners new to both JS and React should wait until core hooks patterns are internalized (~week 5-6, aligning with Qwen3.6-35B-A3B). Do not delay past the mid-point as Qwen3.6-27B's Phase 7 is too late.

contradictions.details: Kimi-K2.7 explicitly delays any state library choice until 'the app actually demonstrates a state-management problem.' Qwen3.6-35B-A3B places Zustand/Redux Toolkit at Phase 5 (mid-path). Qwen3.6-27B says 'defer Redux until after Phase 5.' There's disagreement on whether to actively plan a phase for it or to introduce only on demonstrated need.
contradictions.recommended_resolution: Adopt Kimi-K2.7's 'just-in-time' approach: teach Context API and useReducer thoroughly, then introduce external state management only when a project demonstrably suffers from prop drilling or performance issues. No dedicated 'state management phase' unless the learner needs it.

contradictions.details: Qwen3.6-27B estimates 8-14 weeks at ~10 hrs/week. Qwen3.6-35B-A3B says 8-12 weeks minimum. MiniMax-M3 refuses to give a one-size-fits-all timeline and insists on parameterizing by background/goal/target-flavor. Kimi-K2.7 does not provide a time estimate.
contradictions.recommended_resolution: Provide a parameterized timeline: fast track (experienced JS dev, 6-8 weeks), standard (some JS, 10-14 weeks), beginner (new to JS, 16-20 weeks). Avoid a single number.

missing_coverage: Accessibility (a11y) — mentioned only tangentially by MiniMax-M3 and Qwen3.6-35B-A3B; no concrete a11y learning modules or checklist
Security fundamentals (XSS, CSRF, input sanitization) — only MiniMax-M3 flags this gap; others ignore it entirely
Mobile/React Native fork of the path — all models assume web React; MiniMax-M3 notes the ambiguity but no panelist provides a mobile-specific path
CI/CD and DevOps integration — building is mentioned but automated deployment, environment variables, staging vs. production workflows are absent
Code review / feedback loop mechanisms — MiniMax-M3 advocates human review but no model provides a concrete process (code review checklist, pair programming, mentorship matching)
Debugging strategies and DevTools — React DevTools, browser Profiler, network tab, source maps; no model addresses how to teach debugging React apps
Backend integration depth (API design, REST vs GraphQL, auth patterns beyond Clerk/NextAuth mentions) — treated as incidental rather than a first-class skill
Package management and dependency hygiene (npm vs yarn vs pnpm, audit, lockfile integrity)
Performance monitoring in production (Lighthouse, Web Vitals, bundle analysis) beyond just 'profiling'
Monorepo tooling (Turborepo, Nx) for learners scaling beyond single-app projects
CSS architecture at scale (CSS-in-JS tradeoffs, design tokens, theming) — styling is mentioned but not structured

unique_insights.insight: Frames React learning as an architectural progression map (component mechanics → state flow → ecosystem → production) rather than a flat topic list. Provides concrete anti-patterns to flag (derived state in useState, useEffect driving UI, over-reliance on forwardRef).
The heuristic 'Am I ready for the next phase?' checklist — e.g., 'explain why every useEffect dependency is present' — is uniquely actionable and phase-gated.

Introduces a formal rubric for evaluating any proposed React learning path (audience gate, sequencing, practice ratio, modernity, scope honesty, outcomes). This is meta-useful beyond this single answer.
Explicitly identifies 'React' underspecification as the primary risk and demands clarifying questions about background, target flavor (web vs RN vs SSR), and end-state before delivering any path.
Provides the most concrete project ladder with specific library choices per phase (TanStack Query, Clerk/NextAuth, Prisma+PostgreSQL, Vercel deployment).
Self-assessment checkpoint questions are uniquely practical and testable (e.g., 'Can I build a search input with debounced API calls using only useState/useEffect?').

risks.risk: Panel provides no original user request — synthesizing without knowing the user's background, goal, or context risks producing a path mismatched to actual needs
risks.mitigation: Final response MUST lead with clarifying questions (MiniMax-M3's key recommendation). State assumptions explicitly. Offer multiple parameterized paths.

risks.risk: Contradictory TypeScript timing advice across models could confuse the user or lead to cognitive overload
risks.mitigation: Present a conditional recommendation: TS-from-day-1 for experienced devs, TS-after-core-React (~week 5-6) for beginners. Cite both rationales.

risks.risk: No model covers modern React 19 changes (ref as prop, use() hook, useOptimistic) — all paths are React 18-era
risks.mitigation: Flag that the paths assume React 18/19-compatible APIs. Recommend checking react.dev for the latest API before starting.

risks.risk: Panel ignores how AI coding assistants (Copilot, Cursor, ChatGPT) change the learning dynamic — learners may AI-generate code without understanding it
risks.mitigation: Add a caveat: use AI as a tutor/explainer, not a crutch. The self-assessment checks and 'explain your useEffect dependencies' heuristic mitigate this.

risks.risk: Next.js volatility (App Router, caching, Server Actions still evolving) makes Phase 8 / M6 a moving target — the path ages quickly
risks.mitigation: Tie Next.js phase to a specific stable version (14.x LTS) and note that App Router patterns may require re-learning within 6-12 months.

risks.risk: Qwen3.6-27B's Phase 7 TypeScript introduction is dangerously late — retrofitting TS after building a full component library is known to be painful
risks.mitigation: Reject Phase 7 as the TS introduction point. Earliest is Phase 3-4 (after core hooks), latest is Phase 5 (before the component library rebuild).

recommended_final_strategy: 1. OPEN with clarifying questions (background, goal, timeline, web vs mobile) — do not deliver a non-parameterized path. 2. STRUCTURE the answer as a phased progression map (Kimi-K2.7's architecture-first approach) with concrete projects per phase (Qwen3.6-27B's project ladder). 3. INCLUDE MiniMax-M3's evaluation rubric as a sidebar so users can self-assess any learning resource they encounter. 4. RESOLVE TypeScript timing conditionally: TS-from-day-1 for experienced JS devs, TS-after-core-hooks (~week 5-6) for beginners. 5. RESOLVE state management: teach Context + useReducer thoroughly; introduce Zustand/Redux only on demonstrated need, not as a scheduled phase. 6. PROVIDE time estimates as a range parameterized by background (6-8 weeks fast, 10-14 standard, 16-20 beginner). 7. CLOSE with self-assessment checkpoints (Qwen3.6-35B-A3B), a 'what could go wrong' section (MiniMax-M3), and anti-patterns to flag (Kimi-K2.7). 8. APPEND missing coverage as 'further learning' signposts (a11y, security, DevTools, CI/CD, mobile).
